ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for oat-crusted prawn balls exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this prepared seafood dish.

preparation_method: stir-fried with butter and curry leaves

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of oat-crusted prawn balls is approximately 200 g, comprising 6 to 8 individual prawn balls coated in toasted oat or cereal crumbs and served as a shared dish.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Shrimp, cooked (FDC ID 175180) and Cereals, oats, regular and quick, unenriched, cooked with water (FDC ID 173904), combined with deep-frying fat absorption model
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for oat-crusted prawn balls exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA data for cooked shrimp (~55% by weight), oat/cereal coating (~20%), butter (~10%), and deep-frying oil absorption (~15%), consistent with standard recipe-based nutrient estimation methodology. Energy and macros reflect the composite dish per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of oat-based foods and mixed seafood dishes — analogous estimate based on rolled oats (GI 55, Atkinson et al. 2008) and protein-fat dampening effect from prawns and butter coating
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for oat-crusted prawn balls. GI estimated at 48 based on: (1) rolled oats GI of 55 per University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008); (2) protein and fat content from prawns and butter are well-established to lower the effective GI of mixed dishes by approximately 10–15% relative to the carbohydrate component alone; (3) deep-frying further slows gastric emptying. Composite GI of 48 is a conservative low-GI estimate. GL per serving = (48 × 14.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 13.9, rounded to 14.0, based on 200 g serving with 14.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) from seasoning, butter, and deep-frying process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g per sitting, as a 200 g restaurant serving provides approximately 840 mg sodium, representing over one-third of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from oat/cereal coating combined with high fat content from deep-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low estimated GI of 48 and moderate glycaemic load of 14 per serving suggest acceptable postprandial glucose impact for most individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal, but the high fat content warrants portion control to avoid excess caloric intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter (~4.1 g per 100 g) and cholesterol from prawns (~135 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in small portions, as the combination of saturated fat from butter and dietary cholesterol from prawns may contribute to unfavourable lipid profiles when consumed frequently.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oat-crusted prawn balls (麦片虾球, Mài Piàn Xiā Qiú) is a popular Chinese-Singaporean restaurant dish consisting of whole or halved prawns coated in a crispy layer of toasted rolled oats or cereal flakes, deep-fried until golden, and then tossed in a fragrant butter, curry leaf, and chilli padi mixture. The dish is characterised by a crunchy, nutty exterior from the oat coating contrasting with the tender, juicy prawn interior. It is commonly served as a shared starter or side dish in Chinese seafood restaurants and zi char (煮炒) establishments across Singapore and Malaysia. The oat coating absorbs the aromatic butter and curry leaf flavours during the final toss, creating a rich, savoury, and slightly spicy profile. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein from prawns (~14 g per 100 g), moderate carbohydrate from the oat coating (~14.5 g per 100 g), and notable fat from deep-frying and butter (~11.8 g per 100 g). The estimated glycaemic index of 48 places it in the low-GI category, partly due to the protein and fat content dampening glucose absorption. Sodium content is moderate to high (~420 mg per 100 g), primarily from seasoning and butter. The dish contains common allergens including crustaceans, gluten (oats), egg, and dairy. It is generally considered halal when prepared without pork-derived ingredients. Clinically, portion control is advisable for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or caloric restriction.
common_user_queries: What is mai pian xia qiu?
What allergens are in oat-crusted prawn balls?
Can diabetics eat cereal prawn balls?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for oat-crusted prawn balls (麦片虾球) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA component data for cooked shrimp, oats, butter, and deep-frying fat absorption. GI is an analogous estimate based on rolled oats GI from the University of Sydney GI Database with protein-fat dampening adjustment. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimation. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated values where defensible component data was available. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Grains and staples' to 'Fish and seafood' as the primary ingredient and nutritional character of this dish is seafood (prawns), with the oat coating serving as a secondary component. The cuisine_tag 'Noodle' from the input record is not applicable to this dish and has been disregarded. Price data (SGD 42.00) from the input record is noted but not carried into the output as it is restaurant-specific and not a nutritional attribute. GI category is 'low' (GI 48, which is less than or equal to 55 per Veritas threshold rules). GL per serving calculated as (48 × 14.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 13.9, rounded to 14.0 for a 200 g serving.
